title: Correlated order at the tipping point in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5
classification_notes: GPT error: Unterminated string starting at: line 194 column 24 (char 12170)

paper_analysis.summary: This paper studies the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 using microfabricated hexagonal transport devices to test whether the charge-ordered state spontaneously breaks in-plane rotational symmetry. The main finding is that strain-free samples remain isotropic at all temperatures, while weak uniaxial strain or out-of-plane magnetic field induces strong in-plane transport anisotropy; the authors interpret this with a Ginzburg–Landau model involving bond order and a nearby flux-order instability.

figures.description: Schematic of the tri-directional resistance measurement geometry on a hexagonal CsV3Sb5 microdevice, illustrating the three equivalent in-plane current directions and how anisotropy can arise from strain or magnetic field.
Scanning electron microscopy image of a hexagon-shaped FIB-fabricated device with six contacts and labeled in-plane directions.
Cartoon showing isotropic transport in the pristine state and anisotropic transport under strain or out-of-plane magnetic field.
Temperature dependence of tri-directional resistance for strain-free devices, a strained device, and a device in 9 T magnetic field.
Definition of the three-component order parameters: real bond order Δ and imaginary flux order Δ′, both associated with 2×2 translational symmetry breaking.
Schematic Ginzburg–Landau phase diagram showing regimes with and without time-reversal symmetry breaking as the relative critical temperatures of bond and flux order are tuned.
Order-parameter cuts through the phase diagram showing how Δ′ induces subsidiary Δ and how anisotropy emerges in different scenarios.
Field dependence of transport anisotropy in CsV3Sb5, showing anisotropy growth with magnetic field.
Temperature-field phase diagram of the measured anisotropy, showing onset below TCDW and a lower characteristic scale T′.
Temperature-field-strain phase diagram reproduced from Ginzburg–Landau theory and compared with experiment.

Comparison of the present anisotropy results with prior reports from magnetoresistance, elastoresistance, Kerr effect, NMR, and optical polarization rotation, plus a summary of the authors' interpretation.
figures.likely_source: mixed: compiled experimental literature and authors' data/theory

figures.description: Fabrication workflow and device preparation details for the microstructures.
Single-crystal X-ray diffraction comparison before and after fabrication for device S4.
Resistivity quality metrics such as residual resistivity ratio and Shubnikov–de Haas oscillations.
Strain estimates / temperature dependence of strain-related effects for the different device geometries.
Finite-element simulation of strain profiles in the different microdevice geometries.
Temperature dependence of anisotropy under various magnetic fields, including field-enhanced anisotropy onset.
Magnetoresistance versus temperature showing the onset below TCDW.
Combined strain and magnetic-field response showing enhanced anisotropy.
Angular dependence of magnetoresistance used to exclude field misalignment as the origin of anisotropy.
Field dependence of magnetoresistance along the three current directions, including a shoulder-like feature near 1.6 T.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: The paper reports raw experimental measurements of in-plane resistance / resistivity and magnetoresistance on FIB-fabricated hexagonal CsV3Sb5 microdevices, measured in a PPMS up to 9 T and down to 2 K, with 100 μA AC current. It also reports single-crystal X-ray diffraction measurements before and after fabrication, and SEM imaging of the devices. The methods additionally mention finite-element simulations and Ginzburg–Landau modeling, which are not raw measurements.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The paper states: 'Data that support the findings of this study are available at Zenodo with the access link: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.10076422. Source data are provided with this paper.' It also says that online content includes source data, extended data, supplementary information, and data/code availability statements.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: Raw data are not explicitly described in file-format terms in the provided text. The experimental data originate from transport measurements in a Dynacool PPMS, X-ray diffraction measurements, and SEM imaging. The paper does not specify raw file names, formats, or whether the Zenodo repository contains unprocessed instrument output.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data clearly include extracted resistance ratios, anisotropy ratios such as (R1−R2)/(R1+R2), temperature-dependent anisotropy curves, magnetoresistance curves, strain estimates, and phase diagrams. The text also indicates comparison plots and fitted / interpreted quantities used in the figures.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Yes. The paper explicitly says 'Source data are provided with this paper,' which strongly suggests figure-level source data for the main and extended figures. The figures themselves are largely transport curves, diffraction patterns, SEM images, and simulation outputs.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: The text mentions 'Code availabi...' but the provided excerpt is truncated before the full code availability statement. Therefore, the presence of analysis scripts or notebooks is unknown from the excerpt, although code availability is likely addressed elsewhere in the article.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset appears to mix experimental measurements, derived/processed transport quantities, and simulated/theoretical outputs. The strongest evidence for data semantics is that the paper provides Zenodo-hosted supporting data plus source data with the paper, but the excerpt does not specify whether Zenodo contains raw instrument files, processed CSVs, or both. The methods indicate that some quantities are directly measured (resistance, XRD, SEM) while others are derived (anisotropy ratios, strain estimates, phase diagrams) or simulated (COMSOL strain fields, Ginzburg–Landau phase diagrams).
